The @lyst Index is a quarterly metric that tracks fashion “heat” online, analysing brand influence by combining data from over 160 million shoppers with signals from social media, search, editorial content, and AI-driven discovery.
The Q1 2026 report is built around three key dimensions — Desire, Demand, and Discovery — measuring brand relevance while filtering out noise driven by discounts or short-term traffic, and instead focusing on aspiration-led demand.
As a result, the Top 20 has been reshaped by new momentum across the industry, with #Chanel taking the number one spot. What do you think of this ranking? Swipe to explore the Top 20 brands.

posts.caption:
KOMONO teams up with British designer Maximilian Raynor on a capsule that brings his distinctive visual language into eyewear. First shown during London Fashion Week as part of his SS26 collection “I’ll Cry If I Want To”, the collaboration now lands as a full eyewear release.
Raynor references childhood memories, ghostly birthday scenes, RAF imagery, 1920s silhouettes, and handmade garments passed through generations. It all comes together in a collection that feels like fragments of different times stitched into one narrative.
The capsule is built around two styles, Rope and Core, each in Cognac, Maple, and Black. Clean shapes are offset with subtle metal details and punk-leaning hardware. Small gestures that carry his signature without overpowering the frames.

posts.caption:
History has a peculiar hold over certain places, forever dangling the memory of an earlier, often better time over us. Ibiza is one such place, where different generations will always say theirs was the last to experience it before its supposed ruin. ⁠
⁠
What we can say for certain is that 50 years ago, when German photographer Walter Rudolph went there with his camera in tow, the island – large parts of it, at least – would have been unrecognisable compared to today.⁠
⁠
By the mid-1970s, the kernels of nightlife culture were already there (Pacha opened in 1973) and tourism was on the rise, but Ibiza was still an outpost for bohemians, a few years away from becoming the land of superclubs. “It was just in that sweet spot between still being in the past and being popular,” says @emmasalahi, owner of Ibiza’s @agonyecstasygallery, which is publishing a new book of Rudolph’s photographs that document this juncture in the island’s history. ⁠
⁠
@himeganwilliams speaks with Salahi about Walter Rudolph’s sun-drenched photographs at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 #WalterRudolph: Ibiza 1976 © United Archives, Walter Rudolph, Courtesy of Agony + Ecstasy

Born in Hawick in 1969, Scottish painter @andrew.cranston came to art circuitously – from a small Borders town, via music and manual work, to the Royal College of Art and, eventually, a studio in Glasgow. ⁠
⁠
His paintings, often made on found book covers, unfold like half-remembered stories, shaped by what he calls “creative misremembering”, where domestic scenes slip gently into the uncanny. At the link in bio, the artist walks @pconsuelabh6 through his remarkable new exhibition at @stuartshavemodernart in London 📲⁠
⁠
📸 I’m going in a field by Andrew Cranston. Courtesy the artist and Modern Art © Andrew Cranston. Photo: Modern Art⁠

posts.caption:
@chadmooreholla_ is best known for his tender, spontaneous pictures of New York’s bright young things, but the American photographer’s latest photo book is a departure from his usual portraits. ⁠
⁠
As the title suggests, Eyes and Skies (published by @superlabo), moves between the micro and the macro as it presents his photographs of sunsets and sunrises, skylines and starry nights alongside his close-up studies of eyes. ⁠
⁠
Moore first turned his lens skyward during Covid, creating his previous book Anybody Anyway – a monograph combining his dreamy portraits with pictures of the cosmos. In a sense, Eyes and Skies picks up where Anybody Anyway left off – contemplating the mysteries of the universe. ⁠
⁠
“I started doing the sky images quite slowly, as I matured with making photos and got bored with waiting around for something to happen. Most of my images rely on someone else – typically my friends,” he tells @ohnoitsemilyd. “I just had the thought, ‘What has the same energy as an emotional portrait of a human subject?’, and I figured the closest thing would be the stars.” Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Eyes and Skies by @chadmooreholla_⁠
⁠
#ChadMoore⁠

posts.caption:
Chanel has always been in the business of making fake things feel more real than the “real’ thing. Coco herself started it — not with embroidery or runway spectacle, but with pearls. Fake ones. “I only like fake jewelry,” she said, “because it’s provocative.” the illusion was the statement all along. Trompe l’oeil is french for “deceives the eye” — making something look like what it isn’t.
When Lagerfeld arrived in 1983, his first act was to have Maison Lesage embroider trompe l’oeil jewelry onto a black dress — a witty echo of Coco’s costume pearls, now stitched permanently into the fabric itself. And it didn’t stop there. Those famous Chanel tweeds you know? Not tweed. Fine silk, densely embroidered to look like it. The house’s most iconic material was a 36-year illusion. Lesage has been the hands behind all of it. Founded in 1858, collaborating with Schiaparelli in the 30s, Lagerfeld for decades, and now Blazy — the same atelier, five generations of making one thing look like something else entirely.
Blazy at Bottega Veneta made leather look like denim, lambskin look like flannel, calfskin look like a brown paper bag. He spent three months printing a single jacket to get the exact shade of blue plaid right. And now at chanel, he’s taken it further inward — having Lesage stitch private love letters, lucky dates, lines of poetry into garment linings. Trompe l’oeil you’ll never even see, that’s the through-line. Coco made faux jewelry more radical than real jewelry. Lagerfeld made faux tweed more Chanel than tweed. Blazy is hiding real feeling inside faux surfaces.
The question the house has always been asking: does it matter what something actually is, if it makes you feel something real?

posts.caption:
Founded in 1983 by French designer Josiane Maryse Pividal, Lolita Lempicka exists in a world somewhere between fairytale and femme fatale. Drawing from literary heroines, Renaissance romanticism, and a touch of gothic decadence, the brand became known for its delicately feminine silhouettes; corsetry, sheer layers, velvet, and intricate embroidery, often creating an enchanted feel, with a seductive quality.
By the late 1990s and early 2000s, the house reached cult status with the launch of its iconic fragrance, Lolita Lempicka Eau de Parfum—the apple-shaped bottle becoming as recognizable as the clothes themselves.
The brand actively showed throughout the ’90s into the early 2000s. After its Spring/Summer 2001 show, Lolita Lempicka quietly stepped away from the traditional runway calendar. In 2026, it still lingers on the ~fashion house’s moodboard.

Between walking runways and fronting campaigns internationally to her silver screen debut in ‘Anyone but You’, @charleefraser is a multi-talented woman. But among Fraser’s manifold skills is one talent that she has, until now, kept on the quiet. “I love roller skating,” she shares. “I’m constantly thinking with the work that I do and [my] schedule. I’m a major organiser – such a Capricorn,” she laughs. “[With] my skates on, the only thing I can think about is what I’m doing. And it just really grounds me, brings me back into that present.”
Through the link in bio, see Charlee Fraser photographed at sunrise on the Northern Beaches of Sydney for BAZAAR.

“Oh, man, little baby Charlee,” @charleefraser chuckles as she reflects on her career beginnings. “It was really interesting because I wasn’t looking to model at the time – the opportunity kind of presented itself,” she says of her beginning. By 2016, Fraser had been modelling locally for three years, with early gigs including runway appearances for David Jones, Carla Zampatti and Bec + Bridge. “My agency was like, ‘I think it’s time for you to go overseas. You’ve done everything here. Go spread the wings’,” she says. “At the time, I really felt ready for that.”

posts.caption:
It's strange: The fashion industry is responsible for more than a third of the plastic waste in the ocean. And the trend is rising. And yes, it's primarily the fast-fashion companies, not the luxury brands, but shouldn't the major players, who receive as much attention as Chanel, take a stand instead of clinging to a folkloric, nostalgic view of the ocean that's no longer understandable given the circumstances? Is it really necessary to send plastic fishing nets and shells down the runway without at least a hint of outrage?
Or is it part of such a brand's image to communicate a "don't look up" mentality? I wish they would take more responsibility.

A fun new fashion mood is bringing playful energy back into style, enter ‘Chatpata dressing’. Borrowing its name from the Hindi word used to describe something tangy, spicy, and full of flavour, the aesthetic channels that same boldness and excitement into clothing. Stacked bangles, bold bindis, chunky jewellery, sleek boots, and unexpected pairings come together to create a look that feels expressive, vibrant, and impossible to ignore.

posts.caption:
Founders of small labels are having to grapple with the cracks that have formed within the traditional fashion pipeline. The old path to growth — designing collections for wholesale buyers, lining up financing to scale and dreaming of selling to a conglomerate or going public — is looking precarious.
To survive, some brands are prioritising financial stability over traditional hallmarks of industry success. That can mean embracing slower, but sustainable growth, and retaining complete ownership rather than bringing on investors.
BoF’s Lei Takanashi (@leitakanashi) explores how founders are reshaping growth, often stepping away from global ambitions in favour of slower, founder and customer focused businesses with their own rewards. Read more #linkinbio

posts.caption:
Many in the fashion industry predicted that consumers would come to accept AI imagery as its use in marketing and design became more common, and quality improved. But sentiment has only continued to sour as AI has become an even bigger part of the public discourse.
There are a variety of reasons for these feelings, including AI’s effect on jobs, its impact on the environment, the fact that its training data was used without consent and its potential for concentrating more wealth and power in the hands of tech billionaires.
Fashion and beauty brands hurrying to integrate AI across their businesses can’t afford to dismiss these views; they can have a direct impact on how brands are perceived.
BoF’s Marc Bain (@marcbain_) explores why brands must reckon with these sentiments — or risk being caught in the growing backlash. Read more #linkinbio

posts.caption:
The founder had everything: a Bergdorf window, two seasons of sold-out drops, a customer waiting list and a deck that opened with “the next generation of European luxury.” Six months later, the fund raising process was on hold and the 8th arrondissement bankers had stopped returning calls. Not because the brand was weak, but because every investor who passed on it said the same thing privately: we couldn’t get comfortable with what happens after the hype.
This is not another piece about valuation compression. Founders have heard that speech. What they have not heard, because nobody on either side of the table has an incentive to say it out loud, is something more basic: every person sitting across from you in a capital raise has an agenda. That agenda is legitimate, rational and almost never fully disclosed. Understanding this is not cynicism. It is the single most important thing a founder can do before starting a process.
Olivier Rivard-Cohen explores the hidden dynamics behind investor decisions and what founders need to know—read his full opinion piece at the #linkinbio
📷 @rhode

The beauty industry has seen extraordinary growth and popularity over the last decade, with a flurry of founder-led and celebrity-backed brands disrupting the category and rattling incumbents. Some of the buzziest brands have been snapped up by conglomerates like L’Oréal and Shiseido.
But how do you extend this buzz for the long haul? Labels that have clear sight of who their customer is, create a unique product assortment and marry it with a distinct visual point of view can still resonate with shoppers for many years.
BoF’s latest case study by senior correspondent and special projects editor Daniela Morosini (@danielakmorosini) explores how the likes of The Ordinary, Touchland, Judydoll and Harry’s have created forever brands in a competitive market.

For the global luxury industry, Salone del Mobile in Milan has become a moment where brands look beyond the runway to expand into the broader "lifestyle" economy. At the centre of this intersection is @DimoreStudio, co-founded by Britt Moran (@brittmoranmilano) and @EmilianoSalci — a studio that has defined the aesthetic language for luxury hospitality, retail and private residential projects worldwide.
As luxury conglomerates increasingly pursue the "home" and hospitality categories to drive long-term growth, Britt Moran offers an insider's perspective on why credibility in this space can't be bought — it has to be built.
This week on The BoF Podcast, Moran joins BoF founder @ImranAmed during Milan Design Week to discuss what fashion brands get wrong about design, why physical spaces still win over screens, and how a partnership that began after a romantic relationship ended became one of the most influential studios in the world.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast

posts.caption:
On Iran's islands, an intriguing portrait carries new meaning.
Photographer Hoda Afshar recalls the image as something almost accidental. Working with a medium-format analog camera, she had been adjusting, focusing, waiting. Salimeh stood patiently. Then the wind came and she pressed the shutter.
Afshar has been returning to Iran's southern islands of Hormuz and Qeshm since 2015, photographing the land, its residents and the invisible, esoteric forces that shape life there — the winds, which locals believe to be powerful entities. In Afshar's 2021 portrait, Salimeh's mask, painted with thick eyebrows and a mustache, is part of that belief. It is meant to deceive the spirits, to make her appear male. Women, it is believed, are more vulnerable to harmful winds.
Years later, amid a war between the US, Israel, and Iran, these islands in the Strait of Hormuz are caught in a different kind of bluster. Warships, an ongoing US blockade, and Iranian sea mines now threaten the waters around Hormuz and Qeshm, placing communities long shaped by trade and migration at the center of a global crisis.
Afshar's family still lives on Qeshm. From afar, she hears fragments of what daily life has become: the heavy military presence, the bombings that, as she said one relative put it, "cut through your body like an earthquake."
Her images feel newly charged in this context, poetic portraits unfolding in a landscape now threatened by war.
Tap the link in @cnn bio for more.
📸 : Hoda Afshar

posts.caption:
Curator Jeffrey Uslip wants everyone to know that everything is normal.
"This is the smoothest exhibition I've curated in 30 years," said Uslip, who has been working on the US Pavilion at the @venice.art.biennale 2026, which opens in two weeks.
Uslip was insistent on the point. "I also just want to be clear, because I know how people talk, it's important to know that we have had complete artistic autonomy throughout this process," he said by phone. Later, by email, he emphasized the process to select the artist — the sculptor almaallen — was "exactly the same" as it always was.
There's a reason Uslip was adamant. This year's US Pavilion, which is partly funded by the government, has been plagued by controversies and delays. For months, it was uncertain whether the State Department, whose Bureau of Educational and Cultural Affairs oversees the search, would select an artist to present in Venice at all.

posts.caption:
In the 2000s, Chanel created some of the most iconic and luxurious advertising campaigns in fashion history. The brand focused on elegance, glamour, and cinematic storytelling. One of the most famous campaigns was the 2004 Chanel No. 5 advertisement starring Nicole Kidman and directed by Baz Luhrmann. It was produced like a short Hollywood film, featuring romance, mystery, and high fashion. The campaign showed Chanel not just as a perfume brand, but as a symbol of fantasy, sophistication, and timeless beauty.
Throughout the decade, Chanel’s campaigns often featured famous actresses, luxurious couture, and artistic photography. The visual style was elegant, dramatic, and instantly recognizable. Chanel successfully combined classic French luxury with modern celebrity culture, making its campaigns unforgettable and highly influential in the fashion world.

posts.caption:
The editorial “Horror” by Sofia Sanchez and Mauro Mongiello for Numéro China (December 2015) builds a dense, unsettling visual world that pushes fashion imagery into more psychological territory. Starring Kadri Vahersalu, the series places her in a sequence of staged scenes that deliberately distort conventional ideas of beauty and elegance.
The images feel carefully constructed, but not static. Poses are slightly off, expressions remain restrained, and the settings introduce a quiet sense of unease. What starts as polished quickly shifts into something more ambiguous and harder to read.
Across the series, there’s a clear tension between control and disruption. The fashion stays precise and refined, but it is placed in compositions that feel theatrical and unsettling. Rather than following a narrative, the images work as fragments of mood and atmosphere.
The title “Horror” refers less to a literal story and more to the overall feeling running through the editorial — something subtle, slightly off, and difficult to place.
What stands out most is how fashion is used to shift perception: still rooted in styling and composition, but moving toward something more psychological than purely aesthetic.
⸻

posts.caption:
The SS22 women’s collection by Dries Van Noten feels like a direct response to something fashion has been missing for a while: a real, unapologetic relationship with Color ! Instead of treating color as an accent or a detail, it becomes the starting point, something that shapes mood, movement, and atmosphere from the very beginning.
Inspired in part by the energy of festivals and the visual overload of celebrations like Holi, the collection builds looks that feel alive in motion. Prints overlap, tones clash and harmonize at the same time, and textures intensify the effect rather than soften it. Nothing feels neutral or restrained. It’s a collection that leans fully into saturation and contrast, but always with control underneath the surface.
What stands out most is how rare this kind of approach feels right now. So much of contemporary fashion tends to move toward minimal palettes or safe combinations, where color is used carefully, almost cautiously. Here, it’s the opposite. Color is allowed to take space, to interact, to create tension and joy at the same time.
There’s something genuinely refreshing about seeing clothes that don’t try to tone themselves down. The collection suggests that dressing can still be emotional, that putting on color can change how something feels, not just how it looks. It’s not about being loud for the sake of it, but about rediscovering how powerful color can be when it’s used without hesitation.
In that sense, SS22 doesn’t just present clothing, it reaffirms color as something to experience, not just observe !

posts.caption:
Why did Matthieu Blazy send newsprint down the Chanel Resort ‘27 runway? Who was he referencing and why? Was it an elbow in the rib to John Galliano, the former Christian Dior creative director who has an outsized claim to newsprint fashion?
Contrary to popular belief, Galliano didn’t show newsprint in his infamous ‘hobo chic’ Christian Dior Spring/Summer 2000 collection. It was the ‘Super Fly Girls’ Fall/Winter 2000 show which featured the ‘The Christian Dior Daily’ textile that we all know and love. Needless to say, that print gained cult-fame after Sarah Jessica Parker wore it as Carrie Bradshaw on ‘Sex and the City’ later that year. And that’s on fashion herstory. No, but seriously, it was huge.
Galliano, though, was not the first to do newsprint. Elsa Schiaparelli’s 1935 newsprint, based on actual newspaper clippings of her accomplishments, is the most well-documented early iteration. In her autobiography ‘Shocking Life,’ she wrote about a seaside experience where she saw fishermen’s wives wearing newspapers “twisted into queer shapes of hats.” Upon returning to Paris, she designed the print, commissioned it, and sold it as “blouses, scarves, hats, and all kinds of bathing nonsense.”
It’s sort of curious to clock newsprint in Chanel’s Resort ‘27 collection, which paid homage to Coco Chanel by taking place in Biarritz, the coastal city where she opened her first couture house. It’s no secret that Chanel famously maintained a rivalry with Schiaparelli throughout her career, calling her “that Italian artist who makes clothes.” A 1940’s Vogue editor, Bettina Ballard, once wrote of a ball where Schiaparelli, who had arrived dressed as a surrealist tree, agreed to dance with Chanel who innocently danced her right into a candelabra, setting her costume ablaze.
So, was Blazy extending a long overdue olive branch from Chanel to Schiaparelli? Or was it a taunt? Is there tea on a Blazy/Roseberry friendship of some sort? Either way, the feud between Chanel and Schiaparelli looked to be the elephant in the room (or, on the beach?) at Chanel Resort ‘27.
And speaking of legendary feuds, is it finally time for Ryan Murphy to take on Schiap and Coco?

posts.caption:
Jennifer Rauchet, Pete Hegseth’s wife, has been accused of wearing Temu to the White House Correspondents’ Dinner.
Rauchet’s husband Hegseth knows how to spend money with reckless abandon. His career is marred with scandal, much of which alleges financial mismanagement. An analysis published in March revealed $93.4 billion dollars was spent by the DoD in September alone. $9 million of that total was reportedly spent on “Alaskan king crab and lobster tails.” Additional purchases include almost $100k for a grand piano and $139k for donuts. Hegseth has also been accused of attempting to personally profit off the war he set in motion, has faced financial mismanagement accusations prior to becoming Trump’s Secretary of War, and paid off an undisclosed sex assault victim with $50k in 2017.
But it appears that despite Hegseth’s ability to throw money around, it has not yet occurred to him to set any money aside for his wife’s wardrobe budget, leaving her to scrape for the cheapest available options from overseas fast fashion retailers.
Indeed, the petal pink evening dress that Rauchet wore to the WHCD perfectly matches with a product listed on both Temu and Shein. Styled with the dress, Rauchet wore Steve Madden ‘Kenley’ rhinestone heeled sandals and she carried a floral appliqué bag which can be found on Amazon.
It doesn’t look like this is the first time Rauchet has done this: In November of last year, she attended a state dinner in a gown that matches a product listing on Shein. And, the pink dress she wore to her husband’s swearing in ceremony also perfectly matches a product made by Shein’s brand Motf. Choosing an inexpensive option to conform to a family budget is one thing, but is it not a little ironic to wear outfits imported from overseas while being married to the ‘America First’ Secretary of Defense? And while attending important events, no less?

Predator turned born-again British MAGA stan Russell Brand is under fire (again) after casually admitting on Megyn Kelly’s podcast that he slept with a 16-year-old.
“The plain fact of it is that in Europe and in the United Kingdom, where I’m from, the age of consent is 16, and I did sleep with a 16-year-old when I was 30,” he said. “But when I was 30, I was a very different person. I was a lot younger, and I was an immature 30-year-old.” 🤮
Kelly also raised new sexual assault allegations involving his ex-wife Katy Perry by Ruby Rose, which the singer has denied. “This is probably the old school man in me... I don’t even hear the crime there.”
Brand, 50, is currently facing seven rape and sexual assault charges involving seven women tied to alleged incidents between 1999 and 2009. He has denied all charges.
Meanwhile, he’s spent recent years seemingly aiming for a redemption arc by speaking at multiple Turning Point USA events, cozying up to MAGAts like Dr. Oz and Worm Brain Jr., even receiving a “Global Defender of Freedom” award at Mar-a-Lago last year.
Nothing says moral rebirth quite like rebranding before trial, which begins in October.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There are artists who arrive with noise, and then there are artists like Zayn Malik (@zayn), the kind who never really needed to raise his voice to command attention. Some people have presence; Malik has always had pull. Fifteen years after his debut, and nearly a decade into his solo career, Malik returns to ELLE India, where he first made history in 2018 with his first-ever Indian magazine cover. There’s no forced “full circle” narrative here, no overworked nostalgia. If anything, this moment feels less like a return and more like a continuation — the same artist, only more settled into himself. “I don’t feel like I’m at such a different place in my career,” he says. “I feel like I’m at a different place and point as a human; I’m developing, growing, and learning from life experiences.” That distinction feels important. The artistry was never in question. If anything, it is the life around it that now feels more fully aligned. Head to the 🔗in bio to read the full conversation with our cover star.

Lights, camera, @billieeilish.
At just 24 years old, the 10-time Grammy winner and two-time Oscar winner has existed in the public consciousness for a decade now. But this May, a new concert documentary attempts to capture her star power and stage presence in a new light—through the eyes of legendary director James Cameron, no less. Eilish never thought she’d make another documentary after 2021’s ‘Billie Eilish: The World’s a Little Blurry,’ which closely documented her adolescence and the making of her sensational 2019 debut album. “I’m so private about my actual life now,” Eilish tells ELLE, but when the ‘Avatar’ filmmaker came calling, she knew she had to pick up—and co-direct the film alongside him.
In ELLE’s May 2026 cover story, Eilish opens up about collaborating with Cameron, how her personal and professional relationship with her brother @finneas has evolved, and her activist spirit: “I make art and music to reach people, to help people.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Just when you think fashion might be reaching peak literature, think again. Yes, models have book clubs, style Substackers are pivoting to fiction, and practically every brand with an Ssense presence has dipped its Tabi'ed toes into the world of books.
But while these collaborations usually live at the intersection of trend ”weird girl” novelists, @miumiu is forging its own lane. At Salone del Mobile this month, the brand held the fourth edition of its Literary Club—an event that had a decidedly more academic feel, calling upon scholars and nonfiction writers and academics for an effect that was part MLA conference, part feast for the eyes. (After all, Mrs. Prada is the rare designer who also boasts a PhD.) And while intellectualism can risk becoming an aesthetic, the three-day event—which featured panels discussing novels, plays, and lectures on AI—was a refreshingly analog respite from everything else.
“Books, difficult conversations, lively conversations, textured notepaper, it’s part of this pullback towards something that we’ve lacked,” says writer and academic Katherine Angel. “There is a real hunger among young people for ideas and tools with which to make sense of a maddeningly nonsensical world.”
Head to the link in bio to read the full story.

posts.caption:
We may have a new @chanelofficial It shoe on our hands (and feet, of course).
In 1957, Gabrielle Chanel introduced the cap-toe shoe: a sling-back with a contrasting vamp that would go on to become an enduring house signature. The style has seen countless iterations since, but perhaps none as extreme, or flat-out subversive, as the one @matthieu_blazy presented at the brand’s cruise 2027 show yesterday.
Continuing to strip and rework the Chanel codes, the artistic director showed toe-baring shoes that were intentionally incomplete. The cap-toe went out the window as the design relied solely on what the brand called a “barefoot heel cap”—or, a sandal that undoubtedly left many thinking the models had come out completely shoeless. Is this a harbinger of a new trend to come? Tap the link in bio for the full story.

posts.caption:
At the height of her career, art curator and thinker Koyo Kouoh was chosen to be the curator of the 2026 Venice Biennale. Mere months later, she was diagnosed with cancer and died shortly afterward in May 2025 at the age of 57.
Now, as the international art fair approaches, the organizers have ensured that Kouoh’s vision, and her dynamic legacy in the art world, will still be infused through the festival. Kouoh was the first African woman to curate the Venice Biennale.
“It’s really a family gathering,” says Fatima Bintou Rassoul Sy, a curator and the director of programs at Raw Material Company, a cultural organization that Kouoh founded in Dakar, Senegal, to curate exhibits, provide art education, offer residencies, and bring together artists, curators, and gallerists in an African context. The group will also be participating in the exhibit. “The artists she has chosen are really about people and practices that have been ignored by the global art market for a very long time.”
Read more about Kouoh’s legacy, her diverse vision for the Biennale, and her championing of African artists at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠

What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠

Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! posts.caption:
Being a working woman often feels like you’re swimming against the tide. In 2026, there are still far too few women in positions of power and too many mothers pushed out of work. It’s no wonder gender inequality remains a defining feature of the global economy.
Enter Miranda Priestly, Andy Sachs and Emily Charlton – the holy trinity of female ambition (okay, at least in the film).
The Devil Wears Prada sequel is an antidote to the regressive and increasingly mainstream idea that female ambition should be stamped out. These women, all iconic in their own way, have rich, successful lives and unapologetically love their jobs – even when doing so comes at a cost.
In the link in bio @nikpeach explains why this is so refreshing, and perhaps even more relevant today than it was 20 years ago.

posts.caption:
@chloessevigny is the real deal. For three decades, Sevigny has cut a singular path, both on screen and in fashion, building a body of work that feels as unexpected as it does authentically her own. In Bazaar’s May 2026 issue, the actor talks about her next move—“The Five-Star Weekend,” a drama series adapted from the Elin Hilderbrand novel in which she stars alongside Jennifer Garner. It is not the kind of project that one would immediately associate with Sevigny, but that’s precisely why it also makes sense; she’ll never fit neatly into any box. “My agents sent it to me, and I was like, ‘This is too mainstream. My fans are not going to like it,’” Sevigny says, “but I really feel there’s really something for everybody.”
-

posts.caption: In Biarritz, @matthieu_blazy staged his first @chanelofficial resort collection five seasons into his role. The show opened with the “Little Black Dress”—a reinterpretation of Mademoiselle Chanel’s radical, loose-cut, drop-waist black dress from 1926, which turns 100 this year. As Blazy emphasized backstage, when Mlle. came to Biarritz, she had every source of pleasure and inspiration at her disposal, day or night. “These kinds of different activities led to a lot of the fashion she would come to create,” he said. The collection featured a significant focus on the Chanel logo, scale-like sequin mermaid gowns, a waterproof version of the iconic Chanel flap bag, and more, as guests from around the world—including #MichaelaCoel, #NicoleKidman, #ASAPRocky, and #TildaSwinton—sat front row. At the link in our bio, Bazaar’s Fashion News Director @brookebobb unpacks the frenzy behind the runway show.

posts.caption:
In the late 90s and early 2000s, National Geographic sold T-shirts through its own stores and catalogs that fused striking wildlife photography with clever, fact-driven slogans. Each piece credited the photographer, listed the species in Latin, and shared real animal facts, turning every shirt into a mini documentary.⁠
⁠
Printed on heavyweight blanks with naturally faded tones and finished with original NatGeo tags, these tees have become rare collectibles.⁠
⁠
Which one is your favorite?

posts.caption:
Matthieu Blazy’s latest show proved the hype around him isn’t fading anytime soon. Showing where Gabrielle Chanel once worked, he mixed classic @chanelofficial vibes with a more relaxed, modern feel, think pink denim suits, sporty layers, bold logos, and a diverse cast including older models and a pregnant runway moment. His now-signature €3,100 jeans are becoming a symbol of this new direction, blending luxury with a more easygoing attitude, while pieces like a shimmering mermaid-style gown and newspaper-print suits nodded to Chanel’s history with a playful twist.⁠
⁠
Off the runway, it was just as much a spectacle. Chanel basically took over the city with parties and big-name guests, showing how these destination shows have become fashion’s version of blockbuster events. With ultra-wealthy clients spending big and rival brands planning their own global shows, Chanel is focused on staying desirable long-term, not rushing growth, and keeping all eyes firmly on them.

posts.caption:
Long defined by iconic ’90s kits and Britpop nostalgia, the century-old sportswear label has quietly evolved into a sharp operator in modern streetwear. Its latest move, a collaboration with niche screen-printing project Secondhalf.pdf, shows just how tuned-in it is: instead of chasing mass hype, @umbro is spotlighting underground creators who remix football culture into artful, offbeat pieces. This shift marks a clear departure from its 2000s lull, when lost kit deals pushed it toward safer, nostalgia-driven partnerships.⁠
⁠
Now, Umbro’s resurgence is fueled by smart, culture-first collaborations. Alongside heavyweights like Supreme, Palace, and Slam Jam, it taps emerging designers and subcultural innovators such as JW Customs, LC23, and Adam Jones. The result is a brand that feels genuinely current rather than retro-reliant, proving that in streetwear, relevance comes down to one thing: who you choose to stand next to.

The collection is polished and has a retro feel without feeling costume-ish. The frames are somewhat reminiscent of vintage Cartier and Jean Paul Gaultier styles. ⁠
⁠
With prices ranging from $200 to $250 USD, you can now shop the entire selection on Ray-Ban.

posts.caption:
If you’ve ever felt intimidated walking into a vintage fashion store, then Koroma Archive is the place for you.
Stylist to the stars, model and all-round it-girl @edielibertyrose is on a mission to make vintage fashion less intimidating. That’s why she created @koromaarchive, a physical store in West London where fashion fans can host events, rent or buy designer vintage and above all else, have a truly “unintimidating” fashion experience.
The archive itself is the result of years of collecting vintage pieces — with a little help from her mom’s wardrobe, of course — plus sourcing for her clients like Sienna Spiro, and features everything from vintage Vivienne Westwood to archive JPG.
Hypebae’s managing editor @naviahluwalia stopped by the store to find out more about how Koroma came about, Rose’s process behind sourcing vintage gems and her thoughts on the future of IRL shopping.
Images: Edie Liberty Rose, Koroma Archive, Hypebae, @heyesben

posts.caption:
@dontoliver always knew a number one record was in the cards. “It kind of felt inevitable,” the musician told Hypebeast. It took five studio albums to get here, but ‘Octane’ proved itself to be the instant-classic he always believed he was destined to make. But despite the whirlwind success, he’s thinking about the long road ahead.
“I create music through an old-school lens. Marvin Gaye, Prince, all those guys made classic records that I still listen to today, and I want that same type of longevity.”
In a wide-ranging interview, Toliver lays out his plans for what’s next: “You can’t take your hands off the wheel. You’ve got to understand the responsibility.”
Check out the full Hypebeast Magazine cover story via the link in bio. Available now for order on @hbx.

posts.caption:
The quietest looks are always the loudest statements, and Avneet Kaur has known this for a while now. 🌸
Draped in the deep, unhurried cool of monochromatic indigo, she doesn’t chase the moment. She sets the pace for it. Denim-on-denim, lace against leather, coastal light and shadow, every element dialled into a frequency that’s entirely her own.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this isn’t just a fashion story. It’s a portrait of a generation that leads with composure, one that finds its power not in the loudest room, but in the stillest one. ✨
The industry moves fast. Avneet doesn’t rush. And somehow, she’s always exactly where the moment needs her to be.
Cool, collected, and completely in control, this is the indigo hour. And it belongs to her.

posts.caption:
Man of the Moment — Yashasvi Jaiswal (@yashasvijaiswal28) continues to rise, and we’re here for every second of it.
From dreaming of centuries in blue to turning those visions into reality and now hitting it out of the park as the opener for Rajasthan Royal (@rajasthanroyals) in IPL, his journey is the very definition of hard work meeting opportunity. Powered as much by an unshakable mindset as by his brilliance on the pitch, our cover star opens up about the drive, discipline, and hunger that fuel his remarkable ascent, “I don’t know where I’m going to go, but I want to enjoy the journey.”
Starting out as a cricketing prodigy, now 24, he’s all about keeping his head in the game, stopping every now and then, to savour the simple joys of life. And maybe that is the secret sauce to success, one game at a time.
Here’s raising a toast to the ‘player of the match’- Yashasvi Jaiswal!

These new tables are worth the reservation, the group chat debate, and getting dressed for.

posts.caption:
Skincare-infused makeup feels like the soft life agenda we didn’t know we needed. Because why are we layering 15 products to look “natural”… like it has prerequisites?
Your base should be doing both: covering and caring, glazing and feeding, all while you’re finishing that call.
For the girl who starts skincare at 8:00 and is onto makeup by 8:04, wants the glow without the mental math, and needs her routine to keep up with her calendar and her delusion.
But here’s the question we’re finally asking out loud,
is it actually a smart solution, or are we just in love with the convenience?
We got suspicious, so we asked the experts.

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
L'identità di @Gucci si è formata per accumulo - di visioni, contraddizioni, intuizioni - fino a diventare qualcosa di difficilmente catalogabile ma immediatamente riconoscibile. È questa natura sfuggente e stratificata che @Demna, direttore artistico del brand, ha scelto di mettere a fuoco con #GucciStoria, la mostra inaugurata il 27 aprile a Palazzo Gucci, a #Firenze. Un racconto che non si limita alla celebrazione nostalgica ma costruisce un dialogo serrato tra epoche e linguaggi: dall'artigianalità che ha fondato il mito, alle tensioni con la tecnologia contemporanea, fino ai codici estetici che nel tempo hanno reso Gucci un fenomeno culturale oltre che di moda.
Photo: Gucci

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠

Spring/Summer 2026 has a new focal point: gloves.⁠
⁠
From elbow-grazing silhouettes historically reserved for opera evenings to silk iterations long associated with regal elegance, this traditional accessory returns with effortless, everyday allure. Once an intimidating accessory, houses like @Prada, @Gucci, and @Balenciaga now have us in the palm of their hands (pun intended) with their modern iterations. ⁠
⁠
Unexpected at first, their comeback opens endless styling possibilities. Paired with cropped tees, lightweight knits, or vivid dresses, gloves become that unforeseen pop of chic—the detail that can make or break a look.⁠ Discover how we’re styling the accessory in L’OFFICIEL’s May 2026 issue at the link in bio.⁠

posts.caption:
With fashion’s most important night approaching, we decode “Fashion is Art,” this year’s Met Gala dress code, drawn from the Costume Institute’s theme “Costume Art.” The show explores the dressed body across 5,000 years of art history, underscoring fashion’s long relationship with visual culture.⁠
⁠
In turn, the dress code treats the body as a canvas, where designers translate art, image, and history into form. Expect everything from the body-forward codes of @JeanPaulGaultier and @ThomBrowne to performance-driven moments on the carpet—recalling @AlexanderMcQueen’s Spring/Summer 1999 spray-painted dress on Shalom Harlow and @Coperni’s viral Spring/Summer 2023 @BellaHadid spray dress. The surrealists are also likely to re-emerge, from @Schiaparelli’s sculptural language to possible nods to Galliano-era @Dior.⁠
⁠
Keep swiping for a preview of what to expect on this year’s Met Gala carpet.⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Planes, trains, and automobiles—check, check, and check. @Chanelofficial has staged them all.⁠
⁠
Ahead of @Matthieu_Blazy’s first Chanel Cruise collection in Biarritz tomorrow, we revisit some of the maison’s most unforgettable runways because no one does runways quite like Chanel. From Coco Beach Spring/Summer 2019, where the Grand Palais transformed into a shoreline, to the Spring/Summer 2016 Air Chanel show complete with a branded jet, spectacle has long been part of the house vocabulary.⁠
⁠
When Karl Lagerfeld arrived at Chanel in 1983, he didn’t just reshape the label, he redefined what a fashion show could be. Through Virginie Viard and now Matthieu Blazy, that spirit of transformation endures, each continuing Chanel’s legacy of world-building on the runway. Blazy has already extended that language through immersive sets, from ethereal galaxies to enchanted forests and surreal urban environments.⁠
⁠
Ahead of tomorrow’s Cruise show, revisit Chanel’s most extravagant runways at the link in bio.⁠

Chanel unveiled its Cruise 2026/27 collection, with Matthieu Blazy presenting his first cruise show for the House in Biarritz, the very seaside town where Gabrielle Chanel opened her couture house in 1915. The collection marks a powerful return to the brand’s origins, blending heritage with a fresh modern perspective.
Inspired by the spirit of movement, freedom, and coastal elegance, the collection reimagines Chanel’s iconic codes through relaxed tailoring, nautical influences, shimmering mermaid-like textures, fish-scale sequins, raffia details, and fluid silhouettes designed for effortless sophistication. Classic tweeds were reworked with a lighter, more playful energy, while statement accessories added a bold contemporary edge.
Set against the historic backdrop of Biarritz, the show wasn’t just a runway presentation, it was a tribute to where Chanel style first took shape. With this debut cruise collection, Matthieu Blazy signals a new chapter for the House, balancing timeless Parisian codes with a cinematic seaside fantasy that feels both nostalgic and strikingly modern.

posts.caption:
Range Rover SV Ultra has just been unveiled as the most luxurious and exclusive Range Rover ever created, and it’s strictly invite-only, reserved for a select group of high-value clients rather than open sale. ￼
Positioned as the absolute pinnacle of the brand, the SV Ultra focuses on ultra-bespoke craftsmanship and a deeply curated ownership experience. Every element, from its new Titan Silver finish using real metal pigments to ceramic detailing and precision-built accents, is designed to feel rare and highly personalized rather than mass-produced. ￼
Inside, it pushes luxury into a more immersive direction. The cabin features sustainable rattan wood veneers, ultra-refined materials, and a multi-sensory environment with advanced in-seat audio and tactile technology that allows occupants to not just hear but physically feel sound. ￼
The rear cabin transforms into a private lounge through the SV Signature Suite, offering reclining executive seating, a full-length console, deployable tables, integrated fridge, and crystal glassware, creating a first-class, chauffeur-driven experience. ￼
Performance remains equally strong, powered by a 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 mild-hybrid engine delivering around 395 kW, combining effortless power with refined driving dynamics. ￼
More than just a flagship SUV, the SV Ultra represents Range Rover’s shift toward ultra-private luxury, where access, personalization, and experience define the product as much as the vehicle itself.
@RangeRover #RangeRover #Luxurious

Aman Group has unveiled plans for its first ranch-style retreat in the Texas Hill Country, marking a new direction in the brand’s expansion across the Americas.
The upcoming project introduces a Western-inspired luxury concept that blends Aman’s signature minimalism with the rugged landscape of Texas. Designed as a secluded retreat, the development will feature private residences alongside hospitality offerings, creating an immersive destination rooted in nature, privacy, and outdoor living. The move reflects the brand’s continued push into experiential travel, where space, exclusivity, and connection to the environment are becoming key pillars of modern luxury stays. ￼
The ranch retreat also signals growing momentum within Aman’s Americas pipeline, as the group continues to expand beyond traditional resort settings into lifestyle-driven destinations. By entering the ranch and countryside hospitality space, the brand is positioning itself to attract travelers seeking slower, more nature-focused escapes that still deliver high-end service and design. ￼
Overall, the Texas Hill Country project highlights how luxury hospitality brands are diversifying their portfolios, moving beyond beachfront and urban resorts to create destination-led experiences that combine landscape, architecture, and lifestyle into a single, highly curated environment.

posts.caption:
#AJewelledSymphony
An essential part of North Indian classical music since the 18th century, a tabla’s rhythmic base complements the vocal forms of khayal and thumri. The most significant part of a tabla is the drum, especially the smaller drum called the dayan. It is carved from a single block of dense, hard wood-very often from neem, known for its strength and tight grain. As the tabla is heavy and closely packed, it doesn’t easily absorb vibration. Instead, it supports and reflects it, allowing the sound to remain sharp and defined.
At the centre of the drumhead sits the black syahi, a layered paste made from rice. What’s interesting is that the tabla is not only rhythmic, but also tonal in nature. The presence of the central circle allows the player to strike different areas of the drumhead and draw out distinct sounds.

This month, Vogue did something it had never done before, something most people thought it would probably never do: It put two 76-year-old women on its cover. Sure, they weren’t just any 76-year-olds. They were Meryl Streep and the Vogue overseer Anna Wintour, and they were there in a meta way to discuss the mythology around “The Devil Wears Prada 2,” in which Streep plays a version of Wintour. But still, 76.
The most recent round of fashion shows, which our fashion critic Vanessa Friedman attended last month, were notable not only for the almost total lack of size diversity on the runway, but also for the fact that they were, at the same time, taking one giant step forward when it came to another aspect of inclusivity: Age.
The Chanel show opened with 50-year-old Stephanie Cavalli, who was one of 15 models over 40 on its runway. Bottega Veneta had nine older models. Tom Ford, nine (women and men). Givenchy, eight. Balenciaga, five. Louis Vuitton, four. Representation of women of a certain age has never been higher. What’s going on and why now? At the link in our bio, @vvfriedman delves into the striking shift.

posts.caption:
Before #MirandaPriestly came the real fashion devil: #Cruella, who thanks to oscar winning costume designer Anthony Powell, was redefined as a fashion and film icon in 1996.
Anthony was inspired by #hautecouture, particularly the work of #ThierryMugler. Sharp tailoring, bold silhouettes, and rich runway textures were pushed into parody to reflect #GlennClose’s camp and villainous character. For example, the red feather “flame” dress (see slide 6) uses intricate detailing to create the illusion that she is engulfed in rage. Some of the corsetry was so extreme that Glenn reportedly felt close to fainting during filming.
Famously, Glenn negotiated in her contract to keep all of Cruella’s costumes, something Disney later tried to reconsider after realizing just how expensive and elaborate the designs were. The lasting impact of these outfits shows how costume design has never been the same since.

posts.caption:
Could #Madonna’s stolen 2006 #Gucci jacket by #FridaGiannini spark a quiet revival? We all know and love #TomFord’s Gucci, but at this point it’s been overdone. Could Giannini’s era be the next interesting Gucci archive for true fashion girlies to pull from?
Frida had a difficult role stepping in after Ford, yet her version of Gucci brought stability and a different kind of glamour. This was the Gucci I grew up with and she made the brand feel polished and aspirational. A major part of her impact came through accessories, strengthening Gucci’s position as a leader in handbags and leather goods.
She loved jewel tones and metallics, and her references to the 1970s set the groundwork for what would come from #AlessandroMichele. While Michele pushed those references into maximalism and eccentricity, Frida’s work feels more archival than theatrical.
What did you think of Frida’s @gucci ?

posts.caption:
Seeing #ElsaSchiaparelli’s archive of perfumes at the @vamuseum made me realise…why are (most) luxury perfume bottles so boring now?
#Schiaparelli worked with creative collaborators such as Jean-Michel Frank, #SalvadorDalí, Leonor Fini, and Marcel Vertès to produce bottles and advertisements that were playful, imaginative, and sometimes shocking. Her most famous perfume, Shocking (1937), came in a bottle shaped like a woman’s torso, inspired by #MaeWest. Another example is Snuff, her only men’s perfume, designed in the shape of a pipe (see slide 11)
Today #perfume bottles today feel so minimal, focusing on consistent, recognisable designs. I guess clean, simple bottles are easier to produce, ship, and display, while complex shapes are more expensive to manufacture and more fragile to handle at scale. Culturally, contemporary luxury marketing often prioritises advertising and celebrity endorsements, shifting creativity away from the bottle itself.

These items aren’t just gimmicks; they’re demonstrations of Loewe’s craftsmanship, requiring intricate pattern cutting, moulding, and a high level of attention to detail. It also pays homage to the brand’s Spanish roots, drawing on a Mediterranean aesthetic of fresh produce and wellness. It aligns with a more relaxed, lifestyle-driven idea of luxury.
There’s also a sense of surrealism, where ordinary objects are elevated. That’s why these pieces feel more like wearable art than conventional accessories. Would you buy your 5 a day from @loewe ?

#Opinion | Standup comedian Kaneez Surka @kaneezsurka recently opened up about the "beauty of divorce" on a podcast by Aditi Chandanani @therapywith_aditivc. "Oh you MUST get divorced!" Kaneez quipped, getting Aditi all excited about 'life 2.0'. "When you get divorced, society just drops all expectations of you... they give up on you, and the giving up is the best part!" Kaneez expressed.
Divorce used to be a woman's cue to hide way from society; a label that crept into every introduction. She is just a woman anymore; she is a divorcee. But the women of 2026 are rewriting what it means to walk away from an unfulfilling marriage. They are refusing to carry endings like shame, turning divorce into a cultural statement. Earlier, divorce defined the woman. Now, the woman defines the divorce.
A woman who leaves an unhappy marriage disrupts a comforting myth - that self sacrifice is the highest form of femininity. When she says, “This is not working for me,” she exposes how many others are quietly tolerating what does not work for them either. And that can feel threatening to many.  But here is the truth women know in their bones. Divorce doesn't celebrate heartbreak; it is usually the aftermath of it. So why should women carry the shame of something that often requires immense courage? Why should they be the ones apologising for choosing peace?

posts.caption:
FIFA has permitted Afghan Women United, a squad composed of refugees from around the world, to represent Afghanistan in official competitions. Afghan women last competed in an international game in 2018. When the Taliban came into power in 2021, women were banned from representing the country in sporting tournaments.
FIFA's governance regulations previously prevented it from officially recognising a team unless they were recognised by their member association, in this case the Afghanistan Football Federation.
However, after years of campaigns for FIFA to provide Afghan women recognition and financial support, the Federation finally agreed.  Now, FIFA can approve the registration of a national or representative team "under exceptional circumstances" to ensure players are not prevented from playing international football due to "situations beyond their control".
Read more at www.shethepeople.tv

posts.caption:
The Supreme Court asked as to how a charge of a rape based on a promise of marriage could arise from a long-term live-in relationship where the couple even had a child.
The court was hearing a petition by a woman accusing her former live-in partner of rape and sexual assault on the false pretext of marriage. The woman claimed that the man lured her when she was 18 years old without revealing that he was already married and allegedly had four wives, according to NDTV. He reportedly entered a relationship with her on the promise of marriage. They lived together for over 15 years and even had a child.
The SC bench led by Justice B V Nagarathna questioned, "Why did she go and live with him before marriage? See, now if we ask these questions, they say we are victim-shaming. What is this?... Once he (accused) walks out, does it become a criminal offence?"
The bench further asked, "Why did she go live with him and have a child? Now she is saying sexual assault." The bench referred to the decade-long relationship and questioned the applicability of criminal charges. The bench observed, "So, there was a consensual relationship. They were living together."
Read more at www.shethepeople.tv

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.

Burnout in fashion styling is the most expensive thing in the industry that nobody talks about
It is romanticised as dedication. Mistaken for passion. Disguised as hustle. Most stylists hit a wall and do not have the language for what is happening.
The cost is the dream client missed. The rate that dropped. The quality that started slipping. The
friendships that thinned out. The Sundays that disappeared.
Burnout does not just cost you a few weeks. It costs you the career you were trying to build.
Recovery is real. Take a break. Make time for the things you love. Audit the work that drains you. Ask for help. None of it is optional.

posts.caption:
Priyanka Shah (@pri_ism) is a creative director, stylist, and artist based in Mumbai. Trained as a communication designer, her practice has evolved from graphic design into a richly tactile, multidisciplinary language that sits at the intersection of art, design, craft and narrative building. After practising communication design for seven years, Shah grew creatively restless and quit her job to start a self-initiated project involving foraging botanicals to create plant-based sculptures. “It was instinctive and exploratory. Less about outcome, more about process,” she notes.
It was this impulse toward the tactile that culminated in the founding of Studio Lalala, a practice that is rooted in immersive storytelling. The studio’s body of work now spans installations, creating spatial environments, styling, and designing sculptural objects. At its core, the practice is less about the medium and more about the narrative and approach. “At its core, my work is about storytelling through material. Whether we are creating a window display, styling a shoot, designing an installation, or building absculptural object, the intention is the same: to create an experience that evokes emotion and curiosity.”
Growing up, creativity wasn’t just a hobby for her, but a part of her life, with her father taking her to work and showing her screen printing, offset printing, and various other industrial processes. “My parents always encouraged curiosity and experimentation. Looking back, I realise that my practice is simply an extension of that childhood, of being fascinated by materials, processes, and the magic of making.” This same curiosity now
informs everything she does, whether in installations, object-making, or fantastical environments; the core of the practice is informed by research, process, and deep
exploration. “I really, really enjoy diving into new  materials, learning from artisans, and pushing ourselves into unfamiliar territories. Learning as we go is a fundamental part of the studio’s DNA.”
📸: Priyanka Shah (@pri_ism)

A momo isn’t just a ₹30 street snack. A Manipuri saree isn’t a ₹3,000 knockoff. Sacred shawls aren’t table runners.
When culture is stripped of context, representation slips into appropriation — and the cost runs deeper than money.
North East India is visible, yet still invisibilised. Newspaper coverage? Just 1.48% of space. OTT shows turn lived realities into “tribal survival” spectacles.
As Binita Chamling puts it: “When visibility itself is inaccurate, identity becomes fragile.” This is what erasure looks like — skill lost, nuance flattened, dignity compromised.
Representation requires context, respect, and care. Anything less reduces culture to shorthand.
It’s time to move beyond exoticising, and start paying attention.

Chanel is officially back on top. Under Matthieu Blazy, the house just claimed the #1 spot on the Lyst Index for Q1 2026—a major debut, and notably the first time Chanel has even entered the top 20. Not subtle.
The shift reflects more than just hype. Lyst’s updated ranking now tracks Desire, Demand, and Discovery—and Blazy is hitting all three. His approach has been quietly disruptive: less logo, more material. Less archive worship, more evolution. The clothes feel like they’re moving again.
Blazy’s biggest win isn’t just making it popular, it’s making it feel alive. Like a house in progress, not a museum.
And right now, that’s what people want.
#Chanel #MatthieuBlazy #LystIndex #FashionIndustry

posts.caption:
If you feel like you’ve been seeing Mark Rothko everywhere lately… same.
Across feeds, galleries, and conversations, his work is having a very 2026 moment—and it’s not random. Rothko’s signature color field paintings—those soft, floating blocks of color—were never just visual. They were emotional environments, meant to be felt more than understood.
He was part of the Abstract Expressionist movement, but his work always leaned quieter, more internal. Large-scale canvases that pull you in, asking you to sit with something deeper: tragedy, ecstasy, tension, stillness.
And right now, that kind of emotional space hits differently.
#MarkRothko #AbstractArt #ArtHistory #CulturalMood #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption:
Did you know Luca Guadagnino designed a hotel in Rome? The director behind Call Me by Your Name and Challengers made his hospitality debut with Palazzo Talìa, a 16th-century building transformed into a 26-room boutique hotel through his design firm, Studio Luca Guadagnino.
The concept is very on brand: each space feels like a stage set. A monumental floral carpet by Nigel Peake winds through the entrance, the lobby features Gae Aulenti chairs and a dramatic Murano chandelier, and the bar’s silver-tiled counters reflect original 18th-century frescoes.
Guadagnino’s studio designed the common areas and the Terrace Suite, while the remaining rooms were curated by Mia Home Design Gallery and Laura Feroldi Studio, creating a layered mix of cinematic vision and classic Italian interiors.
A hotel, but make it auteur.
#LucaGuadagnino #Rome #PalazzoTalia #Design #LuxuryTravel

posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire…. Don’t do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me…. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it’s been verified and is authentic…. 🫢 I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it’s got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture… bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They’ve said they can’t send me a picture and can’t provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I’ve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don’t believe it’s real and therefore don’t wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn’t sell at all? I imagine that I won’t see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it’s mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I’ve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I’m going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review…. It’s a good job I guess that it wasn’t something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

posts.caption:
Manoj Bajpayee (@bajpayee.manoj) is ridiculously youthful-looking for a 50-something. He’s trim, his face glows, he’s light on his feet. His body of work is already impressive and curated, and his physical self suggests the same level of care and attention. “You sacrifice a lot and don’t compromise with your daily routine,” says Bajpayee. It’s been 17 years since he started skipping dinners, fitness advice that has now become a habit. His work follows the same rigour.
From the beginning, there’s been a palpable hunger in Bajpayee’s progression—an unwavering journey towards excellence that has been present ever since he became the centrepiece of Ram Gopal Varma’s classic crime drama ‘Satya’ as the flamboyant gangster Bhiku Mhatre in 1998. Over the years, the actor became the name to go to for films and shows that demanded an assiduous unpacking of characters with his ability to not just inhabit a part but also make its impact linger beyond the end credits. Today, he’s at the top of his game, occupying a position that once was the late Irrfan’s, straddling mainstream, indie, and middle-of-the-road projects.
Nila Iyer (@nilaiyer) sits down with Bajpayee to discuss films, family, and his 30-year-long career.

If you have visited Gurugram, it’s not hard to infer that the city is basically a big lineup of cafés and malls on loop, especially around familiar stretches such as Golf Course Road and the Mehrauli-Gurgaon Road.And though most of them pass the Instagram vibe check, hardly any of them have a drool-inducing assemblage of house-baked items on their menu.
At least not till until chef Vanshika Bhatia(@chefvanshikabhatia) opened Gram Street Coffee(@gramstreetcoffee). Bhatia picked Arjun Marg—one of Gurugram’s older, slightly under-the-radar neighbourhoods—as the venue for her newest venture, and made it abundantly clear that she wasn’t gunning to be one of the brick-walled industrial places made common by third-wave coffee spots.
Instead, her 10-seater glass-fronted café is painted in hard-to-miss soft pops of lilac and looks straight out of a K-drama—compact, polished, with chrome cutting through the pastel. One corner is lined with merch, featuring tumblers to sunglasses, while the other is where the real action is—bakes on display, a Carpigiani gelato machine, and a coffee counter.
At the link in bio, read @geetikasachdev’s full review of the café.

“It was only 8 am and I was already on my third plate. A slice of pork cotechino rested on a crumble of almond cake, lifted with zabaglione and a few drops of Massimo Bottura’s(@massimobottura) own aged balsamico. Bottura passed by, glanced at the plate, and said, almost in passing, ‘Careful. You’ll ruin lunch.’
Casa Maria Luigia(@casamarialuigia), in the Italian countryside outside Modena, is not a hotel in the conventional sense. It is a 12-room extension of Bottura’s world, built with his wife Lara Gilmore(@laratgilmore). The walls carry Contemporary art, the kitchen runs as if for family, and breakfast is staged with the same intent as the meal at Osteria Francescana, which most guests will never get into.

posts.caption:
The “Made in India” Prada Kolhapuri, released yesterday into an already bristling conversation on cultural appropriation, reveals something about design but more about the poor choice of promotional language. It also reveals that Western brands may love our “inspirational products” but do not treat India as a viable consumer market. These chappals are crafted, we are told, by artisans associated with the government-backed leather industry bodies of Karnataka and Maharashtra: LIDCOM and LIDKAR. Manufactured in India and developed through a Prada-funded skills initiative, they arrive as “inspired by” the Kolhapuri chappal. The semantic choice is a contradiction. If this is a collaboration involving the National Institute of Fashion Technology (NIFT) and Karnataka Institute of Fashion & Leather Technology (KILT), if the production is rooted in Indian legacy, what exactly is being “inspired”? Could it also be called “How to be inspired by what you have always been doing but learn to move ownership to arm’s length”?
From bandhani skirts to jhumkas by brands like Ralph Lauren—Indian design vocabulary is being repeatedly mined, translated, and elevated elsewhere. But rarely addressed to India as a market with agency. Perhaps this is not just misrepresentation, but the luxury world’s worst kept secret. With the safe assumption that Indian consumers will not be the primary buyers of these products—whether Kolhapuris relabelled by Prada or Janpath jhumkas made in Italy—the Indian buyer is being ticked off as irrelevant and as consumer market—India remains peripheral. Read the full piece via the link in bio.
🖋️ @shefatwork
@lidcom_official @n.i.f.t @lidkarkarnataka
#thevofashion #pradakolhapuri #madeinindia

posts.caption:
“Raghu Rai was my closest friend for the last 55 years. He was a darshanik (philosopher), he created jhankis (tableaus). His photographs were never about one “decisive moment”. He did not think or create two dimensionally. He did not just catch the moment, he would take in an entire energy field. He would orchestrate his photographs for them to happen through that energy field that only he could foresee. Notice a gap in a fence outside Taj Mahal; go wait at a particular place, if a procession were to pass or a crowd was coming his way instead of just holding up the camera from any place, as many others would, to take a photo. So, an image Raghu would capture at the moment or just after a dead body had passed would linger for a very long time, far beyond the life of a momentary photograph. That’s what a tableau is…it is not one image. I have hundreds of photos Raghu took; let me go look for them. I am grieving…”
As told to @shefatwork
🖋️ @therajeevsethi
#thevofashion #raghurai

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com

posts.caption:
I rewatched Anastasia and then spent three hours finding out everything I didn’t know about it as a kid. Fair warning: this film is not historically accurate, and by the end of these slides you’ll understand exactly why that matters.
Also how did we KNOW the blue dress was velvet without knowing it was velvet?
But first, Meg Ryan had to be convinced to take the role, Liz Callaway sang every song you love, and Jim Cummings voiced two of the most iconic villain songs of the 90s without getting nearly enough credit for either.
The further I went, the more complicated it got. The film is built on a rumour that was already disproven before it came out. It gave the revolution a face so the Romanovs didn’t have to have one. And Anastasia was canonised as a saint in 2000, a detail Western nostalgia tends to skip past entirely.
I still love this film. I’m just sitting with it differently now. What’s your favourite part? 🙊
